context: My responsibilities are as follows:

• Identify end-user needs.
• Collaborate with design and development teams to ensure a user-centered approach.
• Conduct user research: surveys, interviews, usability tests, etc.
• Analyze user feedback and make concrete recommendations. 
• Design wireframes and user journeys. 
• Collaborate on documentation and follow-up for UX projects.
• Monitor trends and best practices in UX.
My responsibilities are as follows:

• Co-lead creative direction by applying user-centered design, UX research, and competitive analysis to guide product strategy. 
• Analyzed and translated data from a comprehensive survey of over 300 users to refine user personas and inform key product development priorities.
• Manage end-to-end user testing cycles, from design and execution to analysis, to continuously optimize the product experience.
• Architect a scalable mobile UI in Figma with a robust component system to accelerate future design sprints and reinforce brand consistency.
My responsibilities were as follows:

• Overhauled a critical user workflow using a corrective design methodology to resolve key usability pain points and simplify the user journey. • Designed and administered user testing protocols to identify and address barriers to usage for a more seamless product interaction. • Conducted in-depth thematic analysis of user data, applying Colombo & Pasch's heuristics for a precise interpretation of UX issues. • Defined strategic recommendations for intuitive interfaces in Figma, significantly improving the quality of the learning experience. • Aligned divergent expectations between two major stakeholders through transparent communication, earning praise and sign-off on the final deliverable.

During my first-year internship at the Ecole Nationale Supérieure de Cognitique, I was responsible for supervising second-grade students, who were themselves on an internship, in order to introduce them to the school and its fields of study.

• Developed and led interactive workshops introducing high school students to UX/UI design, wireframing, and prototyping using Figma.
• Taught accessibility principles and human-centered design techniques to foster an inclusive design mindset in young learners.
• Balanced technical content with hands-on activities to enhance participant engagement and improve learning outcomes.
• Simplified complex design concepts into beginner-friendly lessons, improving communication and teaching skills.

projects.title: DeskFriend
context: This project was part of the User-Centered Design (UCD) module at the École Supérieure de Cognitique (Graduate School of Cognitive Engineering). It was structured into three distinct phases, each focusing on a different product. This organization simulated a high-pressure professional environment: the ability to rapidly immerse oneself in a new problem, take over specifications from another team, or test a concept designed by someone else.
Throughout the process, we followed the Double Diamond methodology: Discover (Immersion), Define, Ideate, and Prototype.

sections.title: Discovery, Definition & idéation (Project Ridar)
sections.description: The goal was to reduce bicycle theft. We began with a competitor benchmark and a literature review to identify major market trends.

sections.title: What the data tells us
description: Bicycle theft is primarily an urban, daytime phenomenon (48% occur between 8:00 AM and 6:00 PM) and happens quickly: 50% of thefts occur during stops lasting less than 30 minutes. A striking figure: 87% of stolen bikes were actually locked to a fixed point.
Target Audience Based on this data, we defined our target as regular urban cyclists making frequent, short stops during the day.

sections.title: Field Research
description: We conducted observations and interviews in Bordeaux (Bergonié and Victoire districts) to cross-reference our data with real-world behavior.

Observation Key Findings Out of 18 profiles observed, 56% of bikes were locked. The average locking time was extremely short: 23 seconds. We also noted a high reliance on proximity: 93% of cyclists stayed within 10 meters of their bike. Interview Key Findings The price of the bike radically influences the protection strategy: an old bike is locked carelessly, while an expensive one generates high anxiety. 50% of respondents reported feeling uneasy leaving their bike, even for a minute. The primary need identified was the ability to keep an eye on the bike without the hassle of a heavy lock for 'flash' stops.

sections.title: Ideation through The Purge
description: To generate innovative solutions, we used the Purge method. This individual exercise allowed us to clear out all obvious or preconceived ideas in 15 minutes. We then evaluated and ranked the remaining ideas based on five criteria: feasibility, originality, innovation, efficiency, and cost. From this process, RIDAR was born: a discreet 'phygital' device integrated directly into the seat post.

sections.title: Technical Specifications
description: To ensure a smooth handover to the next team, we documented our findings in a detailed specification sheet, including:

• Three User Personas: Rayan (the delivery driver), Julien (the hurried dad), and Marc (the committed environmentalist), detailing their specific needs and pain points. • System Architecture: GPS, BLE, GSM modules, and an audible alarm. • The Handover: A crucial point where we specified that the app should remain a secondary, discreet support tool to avoid cluttering the user’s workflow (especially for delivery drivers).

sections.title: Design & Prototyping (Project Deskfriend)
description: In this phase, we pivoted to a new topic by taking over the specifications for a 'Smart Desk' designed by another group.

sections.title: The Challenge
description: The initial specifications lacked detail and consistency. To build on a solid foundation, we held a supplementary brainstorming session to re-scope the need: improving the physical well-being (posture) and productivity (focus) of remote workers.

sections.title: My Role (Design Lead)
description: I took full responsibility for the UI design and prototyping in Figma. Beyond the visual design, I mentored my teammates on best practices, including the use of Auto Layout and Component-based design to ensure global consistency.

sections.title: The Solution
description: A remote-control application to manage the workspace: real-time posture analysis, 'move' reminders, acoustic isolation management via physical panels, and a 'Focus Mode' to limit mobile distractions.

sections.title: User testing (Project Smart cart)
description: The final phase involved testing the prototype of a third project: an autonomous, connected supermarket cart.

Testing Protocol (Guerrilla Method): We conducted guerrilla testing at a local supermarket (Leclerc Pessac) with 9 participants. The objective was to validate the usability of key features: connecting to the cart via QR code, managing the shopping list, and mobile payment.

sections.title: Test Results
description: The final phase involved testing the prototype of a third project: an autonomous, connected supermarket cart.

• User Flow Validation: 9/9 tasks successfully completed for connecting to the cart. • Friction Point: The 'Connect' button had low visibility because it was drowned out by promotional visual clutter. • User Behavior: A strong desire for 'paper mimicry'—the ability to check off items as they go, similar to a physical list. • The Human Factor: Despite the innovation, 5 out of 9 testers still preferred the traditional checkout line to maintain social contact.

sections.title: Project Conclusion
description: This module taught me how to navigate uncertainty and remain flexible. Moving from urban security to productivity tools, and finally to retail solutions, reinforced a core truth: while the subject matter changes, the rigor of the User-Centered Design methodology remains the only guarantee of a successful product.

projects.title: Krystal 2025
context: The Krystal is THE sports tournament organized by the AS de Bordeaux INP for the 7 engineering schools of Bordeaux INP and the preparation of the INP.

The people in charge of the event asked me to create the logos, and I accepted without hesitation. The project was a real challenge, because for this edition of Krystal, they had decided to assign movie themes to each school. So each logo had to be adapted to its respective movie, which required more work, but turned out to be the most fun part of the project in the end.
I cannot express how grateful I am to them for giving me the opportunity to participate in such a significant project, I owe them a whole lot.
projects.shortDescription: cinematic visual identity for sports events

projects.title: Mates
context: On this project, I worked on the Logo, Brand, and Flyer Design for people in my school. It was really interesting to be challenged on creating a full, coherent design, not just for an app, but for an entire brand.
I tried to let my creativity flow freely, while still following the well-structured guidelines they gave me, and I’m really thankful for that.

projects.title: EmCo
context: This intensive five-day sprint was the result of a collaboration between the ENSC (Cognitive Engineering School) and the STAPS (Sports Science Department).

The challenge was to collect emotional and cognitive data via non-invasive sensors to provide a reliable, actionable diagnostic tool. Our goal was to design a solution capable of accurately distinguishing between a worker's state of fatigue and stress during a specific task.

sections.title: My Role
sections.description: While I contributed to the literature review, my primary responsibility was leading the entire wireframing and prototyping process. As the team's Figma Lead, I oversaw both the visual and technical design to ensure a rapidly testable prototype, while guiding my teammates on UI/UX best practices.

sections.title: The Team
sections.description: A multidisciplinary group of six members (STAPS and ENSC), combining expertise in sports physiology and cognitive engineering.

sections.title: Methodology
sections.description: We followed the Double Diamond framework (Discover, Define, Ideate, and Prototype). Due to the tight deadline, we performed a major design iteration immediately following our user testing phase.

sections.title: Discovery & definition
description: The first day was dedicated to literature reviews and benchmarking. We analyzed theoretical models (Ekman, Izard, Russell) to understand how to differentiate emotion from cognition—two processes that trigger very similar physiological signals.

We defined a specific target audience: ergonomists. The identified need was to assist them in evaluating the real-time state of office workers through objective data (source data) to supplement their clinical interviews.

sections.title: The Concept
sections.description: Equipping workers with textile sensors (socks for electrodermal activity, a T-shirt for heart rate) and an eye-tracker to transmit real-time data to a dedicated tablet application for the ergonomist.

sections.title: Idéation & prototyping
description: I designed an interface structured around four core modules: Analytics, Equipment Management, History, and Reporting. The main challenge was visually representing the distinction between cognitive states (Flow and Overload charts) and emotional states (Russell’s Circumplex Model, mapping valence vs. arousal).
I integrated advanced features to build user trust in the tool:

• Hybrid Labeling System Used terms like Productive Focus and Critical Overload for immediate readability. • Reliability Indicator A feature that grays out charts if a sensor signal becomes too 'noisy'. • FaceID Authentication To switch from anonymous to identified mode, ensuring GDPR compliance and data security.

sections.title: User testing, analysis & iteration
description: On the third day, we conducted usability testing with ENSC faculty and PhD students using a rigorous protocol: targeted tasks, qualitative feedback (verbatims), and the Attrakdiff questionnaire.
Attrakdiff Results

• Stimulation, Identification, and Attractiveness Very positive scores (1.5; 1.1; 1.6). The interface was perceived as professional, reassuring, and engaging.. • Pragmatic Quality Slightly negative score (-0.5). The interface was judged as too dense and occasionally confusing for first-time users.

Major Iteration The emotional state diagram, originally presented as a heatmap, was completely misunderstood by testers. To resolve this critical pain point, I simplified the visualization to show only the current state while adding a much more intuitive'time slider' to review past data.

sections.title: Conclusion
description: This project highlighted the complexity of distinguishing emotion from cognition—a challenge that remains at the forefront of research. From a UX perspective, it proved that when dealing with abstract data, visual simplicity is the only way to ensure user comprehension. Moving forward, testing with practicing ergonomists would be essential to further refine the relevance of the proposed biometric indicators.
